item.title: nettime
item.description: Technically speaking, nettime was (and remains) a mailing list — one of the oldest on the net — but such a bland description fails to capture what a phenonemon it was: an online salon ...

item.title: nettime alumni and alumnae
item.description: nettime’s contributors over many years have included Phil Agre, Amy Alexander, Cory Arcangel, John Armitage, Inke Arns, Julian Assange, Jeebesh Bagchi, Rachel Baker, Richard ...

item.title: nettime nyms
item.description: Early on <nettime> mailing list was “collaborative text filtering” — what, in the age of social media, we’d call sharing. That kind of function was normal on mailing lists, but <nettime> was unusual because it didn’t have a clearly defined focus (Bruce Sterling once called it “the world’s most world list”).

item.title: obj
item.description: I’ve avoided calling myself an artist because I know lots of them and I’m not one — but every so often I have made things that appear in galleries or museums, that art critics write about, ...

item.title: 1997 > New Monkey City
item.description: “New Monkey City” is a one-of-a-kind phonebook for an imaginary city. The project had three main sources. Theoretically, in college I was deeply influenced by the Soviet literary historian Mikhail Bakhtin.

item.title: 2011 > Cablegate — The Complete Wikipedia Datadump, Vol. 1
item.description: In December 2010, when Amazon booted Wikileaks off of AWS (Amazon Web Services) without any clear or credible explanation, I decided to look into whether their decision was mainly ...

item.title: 1993–94 > Cop Sculpture
item.description: “Cop Sculpture” was an exhibition I mounted with Lincoln Tobier in 1993 — or, as the poster said, “one project, two installations,” at two galleries across the street from each other ...
This collaborative work briefly appeared in the lobby of The Wealth of Nations exhibition at Warsaw’s Centre for Contemporary Art at Ujazdowski Castle (December 1991 – January 1992), ...

item.title: 1992 > Roger Ailes — A Retrospective in Context
item.description: I wrote this essay in 1991/1992, which might seem a lifetime away, but it remains all too relevant. It first appeared in a pamphlet distributed at an exhibition called “Roger Ailes: A Retrospective in Context,” staged in 1992.
